children.name: Nyctibius
children.description: Solitary, nocturnal insectivores with enormous gaping mouths and cryptic plumage that allows them to perch upright on a dead tree stump and resemble a broken branch. Their large yellow eyes close to tiny slits during the day, completing the camouflage.

children.name: Nyctibius griseus
children.description: The most widespread potoo, ranging from Nicaragua through the Amazon Basin to northern Argentina and Uruguay. It is grey-brown with fine black streaks and a relatively small bill. Its eerie, descending whistle is one of the most haunting sounds of the Neotropical night.

children.name: Nyctibius grandis
children.description: The largest potoo, measuring up to 58 centimetres with a huge, gaping mouth that can swallow sizeable prey whole. It ranges from southern Mexico through Central America to the Amazon and Guianas. Its call is a guttural, bovine moan that carries for kilometres through the forest canopy.

children.name: Nyctibius aethereus
children.description: A large, slender potoo with a notably long tail and rich rufous-brown plumage marked with black and buff. It is restricted to the Amazon basin and the Atlantic Forest of South America. Its haunting, wailing song rises and falls in pitch and is a signature sound of the lowland rainforest night.

children.name: Nyctibius jamaicensis
children.description: Formerly considered a subspecies of the Common Potoo, this species ranges from Mexico through Central America and across the Caribbean islands of Jamaica and Hispaniola. It is slightly larger and paler than its southern cousin, with more distinct barring. Its call is a rapid, descending series of notes.

children.name: Nyctibius leucopterus
children.description: A rare and poorly known potoo of the Amazonian lowlands, found in Brazil, Peru, and Colombia. It has a distinctive white patch on the wing, visible in flight. It is smaller than the Great Potoo and has a relatively shorter tail. Very little is known about its ecology and behaviour due to its nocturnal habits and remote habitat.

children.name: Nyctibius bracteatus
children.description: A small, richly coloured potoo found in the lowland rainforests of the western Amazon basin. It has warm rufous-brown plumage with fine black vermiculations, providing exceptional camouflage against dead leaves and tree trunks. It is the smallest potoo species and is rarely seen due to its cryptic appearance and nocturnal habits.

children.name: Nyctibius maculosus
children.description: A montane potoo found in the cloud forests of the eastern Andes from Colombia to Bolivia at elevations between 1,200 and 2,500 metres. It has greyish-brown plumage heavily marked with black spots and streaks, ideal camouflage against moss-covered branches. Its haunting, descending whistle is a characteristic sound of the Andean night.
